tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Within the context of mastering all seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—identify the film school that offers specialized producing education across campuses in New York and Los Angeles as well as through a fully online master’s degree focused on entrepreneurial producing and innovation. Then, drawing from publicly available information about both its on-campus Producing School and its online entrepreneurial producing master’s program, explain: (1) the types of degrees, certificates, and workshops offered on campus; (2) the core stages of production and professional skills emphasized in its producing curriculum; (3) the structure, duration, and tuition model of the online master’s program; and (4) the kinds of hands-on projects and software training students complete in the online degree. Your response should synthesize details from across the institution’s producing offerings.

In the context of mastering the seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—identify the divisions of a prominent U.S. film academy (with campuses in New York and Los Angeles, plus online offerings) that train professionals for (a) overseeing the entire production pipeline as producers and (b) creating the foundational scripts that initiate development. For each relevant school or degree track, explain: the types of degrees or certificates offered, where the programs are available (on-campus and/or online), distinctive hands-on projects or portfolio requirements, notable faculty leadership, and any specific industry-standard software or practical tools students are trained to use. Your answer should compare these divisions and clarify how their curricula align with different stages of the film production process.

A prominent U.S. film school that publishes educational resources on mastering the seven stages of film production—and offers producing degrees and workshops in both New York and Los Angeles—also maintains an official digital privacy policy governing its websites and mobile services. Identify this institution and provide a comprehensive summary of its publicly stated digital privacy practices. Your answer must explain: (1) how it handles personally identifiable information (including when it is collected and whether it is shared), (2) what types of non-personally identifiable information are automatically collected, (3) how cookies are used and what user controls are available, (4) its approach to targeted or interest-based advertising, (5) the security measures it claims to implement (including any reference to encryption), (6) whether it shares personal data with third parties, (7) how users can submit privacy-related questions, and (8) the stated effective date of the policy.

A U.S. military veteran is enrolling in a professional film production program that covers all seven stages of filmmaking—from development through distribution—at a well-known American film academy. To plan financing for tuition and to understand how their personal data will be handled during online enrollment and coursework, the student researches (1) recent federal updates to veterans’ education benefits and (2) the academy’s published digital privacy practices. Based on publicly available information, explain which recent court decision update removed the requirement for veterans to request a dual eligibility assessment between two major GI Bill programs, what new monthly enrollment verification rule took effect in January 2026 (including who it applies to), and the specific time window in 2026 during which institutions may submit or modify participation agreements for a supplemental tuition-support program. Then, summarize the film academy’s stated policies regarding how personally identifiable information is collected, whether such information is shared with third parties, how cookies are used and controlled, what security measures are described for protecting sensitive data, and the official contact method for digital privacy questions.

A prospective filmmaker in the pre-production stage of mastering the 7 stages of film production is considering enrolling in online screenwriting workshops offered by a well-known U.S. film academy with campuses in Los Angeles and New York. The student is also a U.S. veteran exploring education funding options. Based on publicly available information, explain: (1) the academy’s digital privacy practices regarding the collection of personally identifiable information, cookies, data sharing, advertising, and security safeguards; and (2) the current structure of U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs education benefits relevant to such a student, including updates related to the Yellow Ribbon Program for foreign schools, the Rudisill decision, the Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act (including VET TEC), and monthly enrollment verification requirements. Synthesize how these policies and benefits would affect a veteran enrolling in online screenwriting training during the pre-production phase of film production.

A U.S.-based film school that offers programs covering the full arc of filmmaking—from development through post-production—publishes a detailed digital privacy policy governing its websites and mobile services. Meanwhile, the federal agency responsible for administering education benefits to Veterans provides updates on court decisions affecting GI Bill eligibility, Yellow Ribbon Program participation windows, and monthly enrollment verification requirements. Identify these two institutions and explain: (1) how the film school collects, uses, and protects personally identifiable and non-personally identifiable information, including its policies on cookies, advertising, and data sharing; and (2) how the federal agency administers and updates education benefits, including recent changes related to court rulings, enrollment verification, Yellow Ribbon Program agreements, and on-the-job or apprenticeship training support.

In the context of mastering the 7 stages of film production, aspiring filmmakers often rely on both specialized film schools and external funding sources. Identify (1) the prominent New York–based film academy known for hands-on training across the stages of production that publishes a digital privacy statement detailing how it handles personally identifiable information, cookies, advertising, and data security; and (2) the U.S. federal education benefits program that supports Veterans and their families in pursuing education and training (including film programs) under legislation such as the GI Bill. For each institution/program, provide a detailed explanation of their key policies or updates: For the film academy, summarize its policies on collection and sharing of personally identifiable information (PII), its use of cookies and targeted advertising, its data security measures, and how individuals can submit privacy-related questions (including the public contact channel and effective date of the policy). For the federal benefits program, describe recent updates affecting education benefits, including changes related to the Rudisill and Perkins court decisions, enrollment verification requirements introduced in 2026, the Yellow Ribbon Program update window, and any trademark notice associated with the GI Bill. Explain how these elements are relevant to a Veteran pursuing professional training in film production.

Then, drawing from publicly available information about both its on-campus Producing School and its online entrepreneurial producing master’s program, explain: (1) the types of degrees, certificates, and workshops offered on campus; (2) the core stages of production and professional skills emphasized in its producing curriculum; (3) the structure, duration, and tuition model of the online master’s program; and (4) the kinds of hands-on projects and software training students complete in the online degree. Your response should synthesize details from across the institution’s producing offerings.

Emphasis on hands-on, learn-by-doing training and industry-experienced faculty
Online MA duration (one year, three semesters) and delivery format (fully online, mix of synchronous/asynchronous)

Training in Movie Magic Scheduling and Budgeting software

Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation correctly identified as NYFA’s one-year, three-semester fully online master’s program focused on executive-level and entrepreneurial producing
rounds.rationale: The question uses the logic of the seven stages of film production (development through distribution) as a filter to identify a specific producing school without naming it (Deep). It then requires aggregating detailed information from both the general Producing School offerings and the separate online MA program page—degrees, curriculum scope, tuition, structure, projects, and software—ensuring broad cross-page synthesis (Wide).
source_nodes: Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation | Producing School | NYFA
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
The school is New York Film Academy (NYFA), which runs a Producing School on campuses in New York and Los Angeles and offers a fully online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation (EPIC).  

**(1) On‑campus offerings**  
NYFA’s Producing School provides 1‑year and 2‑year conservatory certificates, an AFA, BFA, and MFA in Producing, plus short‑term and 4‑week workshops in producing on both NY and LA campuses.[1][2] Some programs are also offered at its South Beach/Miami location.[1]  

**(2) Curriculum focus**  
Across degrees and certificates, NYFA teaches the seven stages of production—development, financing, pre‑production, production, post‑production, marketing, and distribution—through courses in story and script analysis, budgeting and scheduling, packaging, sales and festival strategy, legal and business affairs, and pitching.[1][3] Emphasis is placed on entrepreneurial skills, deal‑making, leadership, and collaborating with writers, directors, and crew.[1][3]  

**(3) Online MA structure and tuition model**  
The EPIC online MA is a 36‑credit, project‑driven program designed for completion in 1–2 years, delivered fully online in asynchronous and synchronous formats.[4] Tuition is billed per credit (with published per‑credit and estimated total program costs) plus standard fees; students may study part‑time or full‑time.[4][5]  

**(4) Online projects and software**  
EPIC students build a slate of original projects, business plans, pitch decks, and market analyses, often culminating in a “producer’s portfolio.”[4] Coursework integrates industry‑standard tools such as Movie Magic Budgeting and Scheduling and collaborative platforms used for virtual production management.[3][4]  

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Producing School (NY & LA campuses)  
[2] NYFA Programs Overview – Producing Degrees & Workshops  
[3] NYFA Producing Curriculum & Course Descriptions  
[4] NYFA Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation (EPIC)  
[5] NYFA Tuition & Fees – Online Graduate Programs
rounds.answer_b:
The film school is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which offers Producing programs on its New York and Los Angeles campuses and an online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation [1][2][3]. On campus, NYFA’s Producing School offers BFA, MFA, one-year and two-year conservatory programs, as well as workshop formats such as 12-week, 8-week, 4-week, and short-term producing study options; it also lists certificate-oriented intensive training through its conservatory/workshop structure in both major campus locations [1][2].  

Its producing curriculum is built around the full production pipeline: development, financing, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution, while also emphasizing pitching, packaging, budgeting, scheduling, legal/business affairs, collaboration, and leadership for film, television, and new media producers [1][2]. This aligns with NYFA’s focus on mastering all seven stages of film production and preparing students to shepherd projects from concept to audience [1].  

The online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation is a fully online, one-year master’s degree delivered in three terms over about 30 weeks of instruction; tuition is charged at a flat total program rate rather than per credit, with listed costs supplemented by standard institutional fees [3][4]. The program centers on innovation, venture-minded producing, and emerging media entrepreneurship alongside producing craft [3].  

Students complete hands-on work such as developing pitch decks, business plans, budgets, schedules, marketing/distribution strategies, and capstone-style produced projects for film, series, or media ventures; they also receive training with industry tools including budgeting and scheduling software such as Movie Magic Budgeting and Movie Magic Scheduling [2][3][5].

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Producing School / Producing Programs pages: https://www.nyfa.edu/film-school/producing/
2. New York Film Academy, Los Angeles and New York Producing program pages/catalog materials: https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
3. New York Film Academy, Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation: https://www.nyfa.edu/online-ma-entrepreneurial-producing-and-innovation/
4. New York Film Academy, Tuition/Costs for online programs: https://www.nyfa.edu/tuition/
5. New York Film Academy course descriptions/catalog for producing and online MA coursework: https://www.nyfa.edu/course-catalog/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and its Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation, so both pass the DEEP logic check. On WIDTH, both cover campus degrees, the seven stages of production, hands-on emphasis, Movie Magic training, and examples of online projects. However, Agent A incorrectly describes the online MA as 1–2 years and primarily per-credit tuition, missing the specified one-year, three-semester structure and clearer tuition model details. Agent B correctly states it is a one-year program delivered in three terms and better aligns with the required structure description. Although Agent B omits the AFA degree (a minor completeness gap), it more accurately addresses the key structural elements of the online program central to the query. Formatting and clarity are strong in both, but B is slightly more aligned with the checklist’s core structural requirements. Therefore, B wins on completeness and accuracy of critical program details.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy (NYFA) trains professionals for both ends of the creative/production pipeline through its Producing and Screenwriting divisions.

**(a) Producing – overseeing the full production pipeline**

NYFA’s Producing School offers 1‑ and 2‑Year Producing conservatories, AFA, BFA, and MFA in Producing on the New York and Los Angeles campuses, plus a 4‑week and other short workshops; some options are also in South Beach/Miami.[1][2] Online, the school runs the 36‑credit MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation (EPIC), fully online and project‑driven.[3]

Hands‑on requirements include assembling real budgets and schedules, packaging projects, creating marketing and distribution plans, and delivering pitch presentations; students graduate with a producer’s portfolio or project slate.[1][3] They are trained on Movie Magic Budgeting & Scheduling and standard production‑management tools.[1][3] Leadership has included veteran producer Neal Weisman (NY Chair) and industry‑active producers/line producers as core faculty.[1]

The producing curriculum spans all seven stages—development, financing, pre‑production, production, post‑production, marketing, and distribution—emphasizing deal‑making, legal/business affairs, and collaboration with writers and directors.[1][2]

**(b) Screenwriting – creating the foundational scripts**

NYFA’s Screenwriting School provides 1‑Year and 2‑Year conservatory certificates, BFA, and MFA in Screenwriting (NY, LA, and some Miami offerings), plus short intensive workshops and online screenwriting programs.[4][5] Students must complete multiple feature screenplays, TV pilots, series bibles, and revisions, forming a professional writing portfolio.[4][5]

Training centers on concept development, outlines, treatments, character and dialogue, and writing for film, TV, and new media—directly feeding the development stage of production.[4] Screenwriters commonly use industry‑standard formatting software such as Final Draft in coursework.[4] Faculty leadership has included working writers/showrunners and chairs with studio and network credits.[4]

Together, Producing steers projects through all seven stages, while Screenwriting generates the intellectual property that launches development.

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Producing School (NY & LA campuses)  
[2] NYFA Producing Degrees & Workshops Overview  
[3] NYFA Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation (EPIC)  
[4] New York Film Academy – Screenwriting School  
[5] NYFA Screenwriting Degrees, Workshops & Online Programs
rounds.answer_b:
The academy is the New York Film Academy (NYFA). For overseeing the full production pipeline, the relevant division is NYFA’s Producing School; for originating projects at the development stage, it is NYFA’s Screenwriting School, plus NYFA’s fully online MA in Screenwriting for work centered on scripted creation [1][2][3].

NYFA’s Producing School trains producers across development, financing, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution. On campus in New York and Los Angeles, it offers BFA and MFA degrees, one-year and two-year conservatory programs, and shorter workshops such as 12-week, 8-week, 6-week, and 4-week options [1][4]. Students produce short films, television or web content, pitch packages, budgets, schedules, and distribution/marketing materials; training includes industry tools such as Movie Magic Budgeting and Movie Magic Scheduling [1][5]. Public-facing faculty leadership includes producing chair Neal Weisman, whose credits and producing mentorship are highlighted by NYFA [6].

For script creation, NYFA’s Screenwriting School serves the earliest development phase by training writers to generate the stories producers later package and finance. On campus in New York and Los Angeles, it offers BFA and MFA degrees, one-year and two-year conservatories, and short workshops; online, NYFA offers the MA in Screenwriting [2][3][7]. Students build portfolios of feature screenplays, television pilots/specs, short scripts, series bibles, treatments, loglines, and rewritten drafts, with many programs culminating in thesis or portfolio review work [2][7][8]. Online MA students complete a 30-week, three-term curriculum and develop a substantial body of finished scripts in a workshop model [3]. Screenwriting faculty leadership highlighted by NYFA includes chair Nunzio DeFilippis [9]. Together, Producing aligns with end-to-end project execution, while Screenwriting focuses on the foundational development materials that launch production. [1][2]

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Producing School: https://www.nyfa.edu/film-school/producing/
2. New York Film Academy, Screenwriting School: https://www.nyfa.edu/film-school/screenwriting/
3. New York Film Academy, Online Programs: https://www.nyfa.edu/online/
4. New York Film Academy, Producing program offerings/catalog pages: https://www.nyfa.edu/course-catalog/
5. New York Film Academy, Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation: https://www.nyfa.edu/online-ma-entrepreneurial-producing-and-innovation/
6. New York Film Academy, Neal Weisman faculty profile: https://www.nyfa.edu/faculty/neal-weisman/
7. New York Film Academy, Online MA in Screenwriting: https://www.nyfa.edu/online-ma-screenwriting/
8. New York Film Academy, Screenwriting curriculum/course descriptions: https://www.nyfa.edu/course-catalog/
9. New York Film Academy, Nunzio DeFilippis faculty profile: https://www.nyfa.edu/faculty/nunzio-defilippis/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and accurately map Producing to the full seven-stage pipeline and Screenwriting to the development stage (DEEP logic passed by both). On WIDTH, both cover degrees, campuses (NY/LA + online), hands-on projects, Movie Magic software, and at least one named faculty leader (Neal Weisman; Nunzio DeFilippis). However, Agent A provides slightly stronger synthesis of how Producing spans all seven stages and more explicitly frames the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation (EPIC) as project-driven and executive-focused. Agent B includes solid citations and adds the Online MA in Screenwriting structure, but gives less curricular detail about the Producing Online MA (e.g., specific coursework components, portfolio artifacts) and offers a more paragraph-dense presentation. In formatting and scannability, Agent A’s clearer sectional breakdown and tighter comparison create a better user experience. Since both are factually sound and neither has major checklist failures, Agent A wins on presentation, synthesis, and overall helpfulness—not on core accuracy.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

source_nodes: Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Privacy Policy Privacy Policy NYFA and FERPA Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy Digital Web and Mobile Privacy Statement New York Film Academy (NYFA)
rounds.answer_a:
The institution is New York Film Academy (NYFA), which hosts producing programs in New York and Los Angeles and publishes materials on the seven stages of film production. Its official digital privacy policy governs nyfa.edu and related online services.

**(1) Personally identifiable information (PII)**  
NYFA collects PII (such as name, email, phone, mailing address, date of birth, application details, and payment‑related data) when users request information, apply, register, make payments, or otherwise submit forms on its sites or apps.[1][2] PII may be used to provide requested services, process applications, send marketing communications, improve services, and comply with legal obligations.[1] NYFA states it may share PII with service providers, business partners, and government authorities as required by law, but says it does not sell PII in the traditional sense.[1][2]

**(2) Non‑personally identifiable information**  
Automatically collected data include IP address, browser type, operating system, device identifiers, referring/exit pages, clickstream data, and general location/usage statistics gathered via server logs, cookies, web beacons, analytics tools, and similar technologies.[1][2]

**(3) Cookies and user controls**  
NYFA uses cookies and similar technologies for site functionality, analytics, and to remember user preferences.[1] Users can control cookies through browser settings (blocking or deleting them), though some features may not function properly if cookies are disabled.[1]

**(4) Targeted/interest‑based advertising**  
The policy notes use of third‑party analytics and advertising partners that may collect information over time across websites to provide interest‑based or targeted ads; users are directed to browser settings and industry opt‑out tools to limit such tracking.[1][2]

**(5) Security measures**  
NYFA states it uses reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect information and references the use of encryption (e.g., SSL/TLS) for certain transmissions, such as payment or other sensitive data transfers.[1]

**(6) Third‑party sharing**  
Personal data may be shared with contracted vendors (hosting, analytics, payment processing, email), educational partners, and governmental or law‑enforcement entities when legally required.[1][2]

**(7) User contact for privacy questions**  
Users can submit privacy questions or requests via a dedicated privacy or legal email address and mailing address provided in the policy.[1]

**(8) Effective date**  
The policy lists an effective date of May 25, 2018, with notice that updates may occur periodically.[1]

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Privacy Policy (nyfa.edu)  
[2] New York Film Academy – Website Terms & Related Privacy Notices
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), the film school with Producing programs in New York and Los Angeles and publicly posted website/mobile privacy terms [1][2]. NYFA’s privacy policy says it collects personally identifiable information when users voluntarily provide it—for example by filling out forms, requesting information, applying, registering, subscribing, or otherwise interacting with NYFA’s websites and services. Examples include names, contact details, and other submitted information. NYFA states it may use this information to respond to requests, provide services, communicate with users, and for school-related business purposes; it also says it does not sell personal information, though it may share information in the situations described in the policy, such as with service providers or where required by law [3].

For non-personally identifiable information, NYFA says it automatically collects technical and usage data when people use its sites or mobile services. This includes items such as IP address, browser type, device identifiers, operating system, referring pages, pages viewed, dates/times of visits, and other clickstream or analytics information used to administer and improve the services [3].

NYFA explains that it uses cookies and similar tracking technologies to remember preferences, understand site usage, support functionality, and assist with advertising/analytics. Users can generally control cookies through browser settings, including blocking or deleting them, though NYFA notes some site features may not function properly if cookies are disabled [3].

On advertising, NYFA says it may work with third-party advertising or analytics partners that use cookies or similar tools to deliver targeted or interest-based ads and measure campaign effectiveness; users may have opt-out options through browser settings and industry mechanisms identified in the policy [3].

For security, NYFA says it uses commercially reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect personal information, and it specifically references Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) encryption for transmission of sensitive data, while also acknowledging that no internet transmission or storage system is completely secure [3].

Privacy questions can be submitted through the contact information provided in the policy, including NYFA’s designated privacy contact/email listed on the privacy page [3]. The policy’s stated effective date is April 12, 2024 [3].

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Producing School: https://www.nyfa.edu/film-school/producing/
2. New York Film Academy, educational resources on film production: https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/stages-of-film-production/
3. New York Film Academy, Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the institution as the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and referenced its official privacy policy. Both pass the core entity requirement.

Width/Completeness: Both responses cover nearly all checklist items—PII collection (voluntary submission), examples (applications, forms, payments), statement that data is not sold, categories of non-PII (IP, browser, OS, pages viewed, etc.), cookies and browser controls, targeted/interest-based advertising, security safeguards including SSL/encryption, and third-party sharing. However, both fail to provide the specific privacy contact email address and both list an incorrect effective date (ground truth: May 21, 2018; Agent A says May 25, 2018, Agent B says April 12, 2024). Thus, both have factual/detail gaps.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides clearer structure with bold section headers and numbered subsections directly aligned to the eight requested elements, making it more scannable and easier to verify against the prompt. Agent B is more paragraph-heavy and slightly less structured. While both are readable, Agent A better mirrors the query’s numbered requirements and is marginally easier to audit.

Conclusion: Since both contain factual errors (effective date and missing specific contact email), neither qualifies for MUCH_BETTER. Agent A wins on formatting, structure, and slightly tighter alignment with the checklist. Therefore, A_BETTER, with no major difference in factual correctness between the two.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: A U.S. military veteran is enrolling in a professional film production program that covers all seven stages of filmmaking—from development through distribution—at a well-known American film academy. To plan financing for tuition and to understand how their personal data will be handled during online enrollment and coursework, the student researches (1) recent federal updates to veterans’ education benefits and (2) the academy’s published digital privacy practices. Based on publicly available information, explain which recent court decision update removed the requirement for veterans to request a dual eligibility assessment between two major GI Bill programs, what new monthly enrollment verification rule took effect in January 2026 (including who it applies to), and the specific time window in 2026 during which institutions may submit or modify participation agreements for a supplemental tuition-support program. Then, summarize the film academy’s stated policies regarding how personally identifiable information is collected, whether such information is shared with third parties, how cookies are used and controlled, what security measures are described for protecting sensitive data, and the official contact method for digital privacy questions.
checklist_width: Identification and explanation of the Rudisill-Perkins court decision update removing the need to request dual MGIB/Post-9/11 GI Bill eligibility review
Description of the January 2026 monthly enrollment verification requirement and inclusion of Chapter 35 beneficiaries
Correct March 15 to May 15, 2026 window for new or modified Yellow Ribbon Program agreements
Explanation that personally identifiable information (PII) is collected only when voluntarily provided (e.g., academic records access, transactions, mailing lists)
Statement that PII is not shared, sold, or rented to third parties
Description of cookie usage for preferences and usage statistics, and the ability to block or delete cookies via browser settings
Mention of security measures including administrative, technical, and physical safeguards and encryption for sensitive data
Provision of the official privacy contact email address
checklist_depth: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs education benefits updates page (including Rudisill-Perkins decision, Yellow Ribbon Program window, and enrollment verification rule) + proof via reference to MGIB/Post-9/11 GI Bill policy changes
New York Film Academy Digital Privacy Statement + proof via references to PII handling, cookies, non-sharing policy, security measures, and listed privacy contact email
rounds.rationale: The question uses a realistic scenario—a veteran enrolling in a comprehensive film production program—to logically connect federal veterans’ education benefit updates with a film school’s digital privacy practices (Deep reasoning). It does not name the specific pages but instead requires identifying the correct federal benefits authority and the specific film academy’s privacy policy through contextual clues. The task demands aggregation of multiple distinct facts: court rulings, verification rules, program deadlines, and numerous privacy provisions (Wide scope), ensuring the respondent must synthesize information from both a federal benefits source and the film academy’s official privacy statement.
source_nodes: Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: VA » Veterans Benefits Administration » Education and Training Education and Training VA is implementing court decisions that allow additional entitlement to education benefit
rounds.answer_a:
In this scenario, the veteran is most likely attending New York Film Academy (NYFA), a U.S. film school with producing programs in New York and Los Angeles that teaches the seven stages of film production and maintains a published digital privacy policy.[5]

**(1) Federal veterans’ education benefit updates**

A recent change stemming from the 2023 PACT Act implementation and subsequent VA guidance removed the need for veterans to request a “dual eligibility” assessment between the Montgomery GI Bill (MGIB, Chapter 30) and the Post‑9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33); VA now automatically evaluates which benefit is more advantageous rather than requiring a separate dual‑eligibility request.[1][2]

Effective January 2026, VA will require monthly enrollment verification for certain GI Bill recipients: students using the Post‑9/11 GI Bill for non‑college degree (NCD) programs and other vocational / non‑standard terms must confirm each month that they remained enrolled, typically by text or email, similar to the current verification already in place for some groups.[1][3]

For the supplemental tuition‑support program (the Yellow Ribbon Program), VA’s 2026–27 academic year guidance states that institutions may submit new or modified Yellow Ribbon participation agreements to VA only during a defined filing window in early 2026; for that year, the window runs from January 1 through March 15, 2026.[3][4]

**(2) New York Film Academy digital privacy practices**

NYFA’s privacy policy states it collects personally identifiable information (PII)—such as name, contact details, date of birth, application and enrollment data, and payment‑related information—when users request info, apply, enroll, or make online payments.[5] PII is used to provide services, process applications, communicate, and meet legal or administrative obligations and may be shared with service providers (e.g., payment processors, hosting, analytics), educational partners, and government or law‑enforcement agencies when required; NYFA states it does not “sell” PII in the traditional sense.[5]

Non‑PII (IP address, device type, browser, pages visited, and similar usage data) is automatically collected via cookies, log files, and analytics tools.[5] Cookies support functionality, preferences, and analytics; users can restrict or delete cookies via browser settings, though some features may be impaired.[5]

NYFA describes using reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including encrypted (SSL/TLS) transmission of sensitive data such as payment information.[5] Privacy questions can be sent to the dedicated email address listed in the policy (privacy@nyfa.edu) or by mail to the address provided on the privacy page.[5]

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs – GI Bill updates and PACT Act guidance  
[2] VA Education Service – Dual entitlement and benefit comparison information  
[3] VA – Enrollment Verification FAQs and policy updates  
[4] VA Yellow Ribbon Program – School participation guidance, academic year 2026–27  
[5] New York Film Academy – Privacy Policy (nyfa.edu)
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is the New York Film Academy (NYFA). For the veteran-benefits portion, the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs states that, following the U.S. Supreme Court’s April 2024 decision in **Rudisill v. McDonough**, veterans no longer need to request a dual eligibility assessment between the Montgomery GI Bill and the Post-9/11 GI Bill; VA now determines the maximum benefit automatically under the ruling’s framework [1]. VA also announced a new **monthly enrollment verification** requirement effective **January 2026** for students using **Chapter 31 Veteran Readiness and Employment (VR&E)** benefits who attend school or training, aligning them with verification processes already familiar in other benefit contexts [2]. For the **Yellow Ribbon Program**, VA says schools may submit new or revised participation agreements for the **2026–2027 academic year from March 17, 2025 through May 15, 2026** [3].

On privacy, NYFA’s published digital privacy policy says personally identifiable information is collected when users voluntarily provide it through actions such as filling out inquiry forms, requesting information, registering, applying, subscribing, or otherwise interacting with NYFA’s websites and mobile services [4]. The policy says NYFA may use that information to respond to requests, provide services, communicate with users, and support institutional operations. It also states NYFA **does not sell** personal information, but it **may share** personal data with service providers, affiliated parties, or when legally required or otherwise described in the policy [4].

NYFA says it automatically collects certain non-personal or technical data through cookies and similar tools, and uses cookies to support site functionality, remember preferences, analyze traffic, and assist advertising or analytics activities [4]. Users can control cookies through browser settings, including blocking or deleting them, although NYFA warns some site features may not function properly if cookies are disabled [4].

For security, NYFA says it uses commercially reasonable **administrative, technical, and physical safeguards** to protect personal information and specifically references **Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) encryption** for transmission of sensitive data, while acknowledging that no online system is completely secure [4]. Privacy-related questions are directed through the contact information provided in the policy, including NYFA’s listed email: **privacy@nyfa.edu** [4].

## References
1. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, “The Rudisill decision and what it means for GI Bill benefits”: https://www.va.gov/resources/the-rudisill-decision-and-what-it-means-for-gi-bill-benefits/
2. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, School Certifying Official resources / enrollment verification updates: https://www.va.gov/education/school-administrators/
3. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Yellow Ribbon Program participation timelines: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/post-9-11/yellow-ribbon-program/
4. New York Film Academy, Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the film academy as New York Film Academy and referenced VA education updates and NYFA’s privacy policy, so neither failed core entity identification.

Width/Completeness: Both responses contain major factual inaccuracies. 
- On the court decision: Agent B correctly identifies Rudisill v. McDonough, while Agent A attributes the change to PACT Act implementation and does not clearly identify the Rudisill-Perkins decision. 
- On monthly enrollment verification: Both are incorrect. Agent A limits it to certain Post-9/11/NCD students and omits Chapter 35 beneficiaries. Agent B incorrectly applies the new January 2026 requirement to Chapter 31 VR&E instead of including Chapter 35 beneficiaries as required.
- On the Yellow Ribbon window: Both are wrong. Agent A gives January 1–March 15, 2026; Agent B gives March 17, 2025–May 15, 2026. The correct window is March 15–May 15, 2026.
- On privacy: Both state that NYFA may share PII with service providers or affiliates, which conflicts with the checklist requirement that PII is not shared, sold, or rented to third parties. This is a major checklist failure.

Because both agents miss multiple critical checklist items (court framing, verification applicability, Yellow Ribbon dates, and non-sharing of PII), they each fail on WIDE aggregation, and Agent A also partially fails on the specific court identification. 

Presentation-wise, both are well-structured and readable, with clear headings and citations. However, accuracy is foundational, and both contain substantial factual errors across multiple required elements.

Therefore, this is a LOW-QUALITY TIE: both responses significantly fail key checklist requirements.

rounds.question: A prospective filmmaker in the pre-production stage of mastering the 7 stages of film production is considering enrolling in online screenwriting workshops offered by a well-known U.S. film academy with campuses in Los Angeles and New York. The student is also a U.S. veteran exploring education funding options. Based on publicly available information, explain: (1) the academy’s digital privacy practices regarding the collection of personally identifiable information, cookies, data sharing, advertising, and security safeguards; and (2) the current structure of U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs education benefits relevant to such a student, including updates related to the Yellow Ribbon Program for foreign schools, the Rudisill decision, the Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act (including VET TEC), and monthly enrollment verification requirements. Synthesize how these policies and benefits would affect a veteran enrolling in online screenwriting training during the pre-production phase of film production.

Description of situations where PII may be requested (e.g., academic records access, mailing lists, payment transactions)
Statement that PII is not sold, rented, or shared with third parties and is handled in compliance with federal/state laws
Description of non-personally identifiable data collected (IP address, browser type, pages visited, usage data)
Explanation of cookie usage and user ability to block or delete cookies
Description of targeted or interest-based advertising using third-party vendors
Outline of security measures including physical, technical, administrative safeguards and encryption for sensitive data
Explanation of Yellow Ribbon Program open season for foreign schools and annual agreement adjustments due to currency fluctuations
Summary of the Rudisill decision regarding eligibility for additional GI Bill benefits when qualifying under multiple service periods
Reference to the Senator Elizabeth Dole Act and the new iteration of the VET TEC program
Statement that most GI Bill students must verify enrollment monthly
Connection of these benefits to funding film or screenwriting education
checklist_depth: Identification of the New York Film Academy as the film school offering online screenwriting workshops (logic: pre-production training in screenwriting at a bi-coastal U.S. film academy)
Identification of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) and its GI Bill education benefits programs (logic: veteran funding pathway for film education)
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by referencing the pre-production stage (screenwriting) and a bi-coastal U.S. film academy without initially naming the specific documents, requiring identification of the New York Film Academy and VA education benefits. It then demands wide aggregation by requiring synthesis of multiple policy elements: detailed digital privacy practices from the academy and multiple VA education updates (Yellow Ribbon, Rudisill, Dole Act/VET TEC, enrollment verification). Answering fully requires consulting and integrating information across institutional privacy disclosures and federal veterans’ education benefits sources.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Privacy Policy Privacy Policy NYFA and FERPA Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy Digital Web and Mobile Privacy Statement New York Film Academy (NYFA)
rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy (NYFA)—which offers online screenwriting workshops and has campuses in Los Angeles and New York—governs its sites and online services with a digital privacy policy covering personal data, cookies, and security.

**(1) NYFA digital privacy practices**

NYFA collects personally identifiable information (PII)—such as name, email, address, phone number, date of birth, application details, and payment information—when users request information, apply, register, make payments, or otherwise submit forms online.[1] This PII is used to process requests, manage enrollment, communicate about programs, conduct marketing (where permitted), and meet legal obligations.[1]  

Non‑personally identifiable information is automatically gathered via cookies, web beacons, and analytics tools, including IP address, device identifiers, browser/OS, pages visited, and usage patterns.[1] Cookies support site functionality, analytics, and remembering preferences; users can block or delete cookies through browser settings, though some features may be impaired.[1]  

NYFA states it may share PII with contracted service providers (hosting, analytics, payment processors, email services), educational partners, and governmental or law‑enforcement agencies when required by law, and notes use of third‑party analytics/advertising partners that may support interest‑based ads; users are directed to browser and industry opt‑out tools.[1] NYFA claims to employ “reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards,” including encryption (e.g., SSL/TLS) for certain data transmissions, such as payments.[1]  

**(2) VA education benefits and how they affect an online screenwriting student**

VA education benefits (Post‑9/11 GI Bill and others) can fund online training if the program is VA‑approved.[2] Recent policy updates include:  

- **Yellow Ribbon for foreign schools:** VA expanded Yellow Ribbon eligibility to some foreign schools, but this mainly affects attendance abroad; NYFA’s U.S. campuses and online offerings use standard GI Bill rules.[3]  
- **Rudisill decision:** The 2024 Supreme Court ruling in Rudisill v. McDonough may allow eligible veterans with multiple periods of service to use more than 36 months of combined benefits (up to 48), potentially extending how long they can study (e.g., multiple NYFA programs).[4]  
- **Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act:** Among other changes, it reauthorizes and modifies technology training benefits like VET TEC, which funds short‑term high‑tech training; while VET TEC focuses on tech fields, its continuation shows VA’s support for nontraditional, skills‑driven programs.[5]  
- **Monthly enrollment verification:** Students using Post‑9/11 GI Bill for non‑college-degree and certificate programs must verify enrollment monthly (often by text or email) to keep housing payments flowing.[2]  

For a veteran taking NYFA’s online screenwriting workshops, these policies mean: they must ensure the specific workshop is GI‑Bill‑eligible (most short non‑degree online workshops are not), understand that Yellow Ribbon generally doesn’t apply unless attending a foreign school, may benefit from expanded entitlement under Rudisill if they have qualifying service, and must comply with monthly verification for any covered program to receive full housing and tuition payments. Meanwhile, any data submitted during inquiry or enrollment will be handled under NYFA’s privacy practices, including possible sharing with service providers and use of cookies and analytics on its digital platforms.[1]

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy, “Privacy Policy,” nyfa.edu.  
[2] U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, “Education and Training – GI Bill benefits.”  
[3] U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, “Yellow Ribbon Program for Post‑9/11 GI Bill.”  
[4] U.S. Supreme Court, Rudisill v. McDonough, 602 U.S. ___ (2024); VA guidance on implementation.  
[5] U.S. Congress / VA summaries of the Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act, including VET TEC provisions.
rounds.answer_b:
The academy is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which offers online screenwriting workshops and on-campus programs in Los Angeles and New York [1][2]. NYFA’s privacy policy states that it collects personally identifiable information when users voluntarily submit it through inquiries, applications, registrations, subscriptions, or other website/mobile interactions. This can include contact and application-related data. NYFA says it uses such information to respond to requests, provide services, communicate with users, and support school operations; it does not sell personal information, but may share it with service providers, affiliates, legal authorities, or in business-transfer situations described in the policy [3]. NYFA also automatically collects non-personal technical data such as IP address, browser/device information, operating system, pages viewed, referring URLs, and usage analytics [3]. Cookies and similar tools are used for site functionality, preferences, analytics, and advertising, with users able to manage or disable cookies in browser settings, though some features may be affected [3]. The policy also notes interest-based advertising through third-party partners and points users to available opt-out mechanisms [3]. For security, NYFA says it uses administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including SSL encryption for sensitive transmissions, while acknowledging no system is fully secure [3].

For a U.S. veteran considering NYFA online screenwriting study during the pre-production stage, VA benefits policy is important but currently constrained. The VA states that effective August 1, 2024, students at foreign schools can no longer receive Yellow Ribbon benefits, including for degree programs previously eligible; this matters because NYFA is a U.S.-based institution with campuses in New York and Los Angeles, but veterans should confirm whether the specific online offering and school approval status fit VA rules for payment eligibility [4]. Following the Supreme Court’s Rudisill decision, VA began allowing some veterans with multiple periods of qualifying service to receive up to 48 months of combined GI Bill benefits, depending on individual circumstances [5]. The Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act brought several changes, including a VET TEC extension through April 1, 2026, subject to available funding, which may expand short-form training options for some veterans, though eligibility depends on program approval [6][7]. VA also requires monthly enrollment verification for students receiving MHA and/or kicker payments under Chapters 30, 33, 35, and 1606; students can verify by text, email, or phone, and failure to do so can delay housing payments [8]. Practically, a veteran enrolling in online screenwriting should review NYFA privacy terms before submitting data and confirm VA approval, benefit chapter, enrollment verification duties, and non-Yellow Ribbon funding exposure.

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Online Screenwriting Workshops: https://www.nyfa.edu/film-school/screenwriting/
2. New York Film Academy, Campuses: https://www.nyfa.edu/
3. New York Film Academy, Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
4. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Yellow Ribbon Program changes for foreign schools: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/post-9-11/yellow-ribbon-program/
5. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Rudisill decision and GI Bill eligibility updates: https://www.va.gov/resources/montgomery-gi-bill-and-post-911-gi-bill-changes/
6. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Senator Elizabeth Dole 21st Century Veterans Healthcare and Benefits Improvement Act updates: https://www.va.gov/resources/the-senator-elizabeth-dole-21st-century-veterans-healthcare-and-benefits-improvement-act/
7. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, VET TEC: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/how-to-use-benefits/vet-tec/
8. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Monthly enrollment verification: https://www.va.gov/education/verify-school-enrollment/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) education benefits framework, including the GI Bill, Rudisill decision, Yellow Ribbon Program, VET TEC, and enrollment verification. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses cover most checklist items (PII collection, cookies, non-personal data, targeted advertising, safeguards, Rudisill, Dole Act/VET TEC, monthly verification, and application to online screenwriting). However, Agent B more clearly states that PII is collected when voluntarily submitted and explicitly notes that NYFA does not sell personal information—an important checklist element that Agent A omits. Both are somewhat incomplete regarding detailed Yellow Ribbon “open season” and currency-adjustment mechanics for foreign schools, but neither commits a major hallucination.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A has slightly better visual structure with headers and bullet points, improving scannability. However, Agent B provides denser grounding (more citations), clearer privacy language (especially around data selling and voluntary submission), and more precise VA policy framing (including specific benefit chapters for monthly verification). The overall informational completeness and clarity give Agent B the edge.

Conclusion: Both are strong and accurate, but Agent B offers a slightly more comprehensive and policy-precise response, so B wins on overall user utility.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy (NYFA) is the U.S.-based film school, and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is the federal agency administering Veterans’ education benefits.

NYFA’s digital privacy policy applies to its websites and mobile services. It collects personally identifiable information (PII)—such as name, address, email, phone number, date of birth, application and enrollment details, and payment information—when users request information, apply, register, make payments, or otherwise submit forms online.[1] PII is used to provide requested services, process applications and payments, manage enrollment and communications, conduct marketing (where permitted), and comply with legal obligations.[1]  

NYFA also automatically collects non‑personally identifiable information via cookies, web beacons, log files, and analytics tools, including IP address, browser and device type, operating system, pages visited, referring URLs, and general location/usage data.[1] Cookies support site functionality, preferences, and analytics; users can restrict or delete cookies through browser settings, though some features may not work correctly if cookies are disabled.[1]  

The policy notes NYFA’s use of third‑party analytics and advertising partners that may support interest‑based/behavioral advertising; users are directed to browser controls and industry opt‑out mechanisms.[1] PII may be shared with service providers (hosting, analytics, payment processors, email vendors), educational partners, and government or law‑enforcement entities when legally required; NYFA states it does not “sell” personal data as the term is commonly used.[1] The school claims to use “reasonable” administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including encryption (e.g., SSL/TLS) for certain transmissions such as payments.[1]

The VA administers and updates education benefits programs such as the Post‑9/11 GI Bill, Montgomery GI Bill, and related initiatives.[2] It periodically issues policy updates in response to court decisions—most notably the 2024 Supreme Court decision in Rudisill v. McDonough, which affects how multiple periods of service may combine to extend benefit entitlement, and VA is implementing revised eligibility guidance accordingly.[3]  

VA manages Yellow Ribbon Program participation through annual agreements with schools; institutions must sign each academic year’s Yellow Ribbon Agreement during VA’s defined window, and VA publishes participating schools and contribution limits.[4] For Post‑9/11 GI Bill students in many programs, VA now requires monthly enrollment verification (by text, email, or calling the Education Call Center) to continue housing allowance payments.[5]  

VA also supports on‑the‑job (OJT) and apprenticeship training by paying a sliding‑scale monthly benefit directly to Veterans while employers pay wages, with benefit levels decreasing as the trainee’s earnings and experience rise.[2] Updates to these benefits are communicated via VA.gov, Federal Register notices, and policy letters to schools and Veterans.[2][3][4][5]

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy, “Privacy Policy,” nyfa.edu.  
[2] U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, “Education and Training – GI Bill” and “On-the-Job and Apprenticeship Training.”  
[3] VA, “Rudisill v. McDonough – GI Bill Updates” (VA news/policy page).  
[4] VA, “Yellow Ribbon Program – Find a School & Participation Rules,” va.gov.  
[5] VA, “Post‑9/11 GI Bill Monthly Enrollment Verification” guidance, va.gov.
rounds.answer_b:
The two institutions are the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) [1][2]. NYFA’s privacy policy states that it collects personally identifiable information when users voluntarily submit it through inquiries, applications, registrations, subscriptions, or other interactions with its websites and mobile services. This can include names, contact details, and other submitted data, which NYFA says it uses to respond to requests, provide services, communicate with users, and support institutional operations [1]. NYFA also automatically collects non-personally identifiable information such as IP address, browser and device information, operating system, usage patterns, pages viewed, referring URLs, and similar analytics data to administer and improve its digital services [1].

NYFA says it uses cookies and related technologies to remember preferences, support site functionality, analyze traffic, and assist with advertising. Users can manage or disable cookies through browser settings, though some features may not function properly if cookies are blocked [1]. The school also discloses the use of targeted or interest-based advertising through third-party partners and points users to available opt-out mechanisms [1]. On data sharing, NYFA says it does not sell personal information, but may share it with service providers, affiliates, legal authorities, or in connection with business transfers as described in the policy [1]. For protection, it states that it uses administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including SSL encryption for sensitive transmissions, while acknowledging that no system is completely secure [1].

The VA administers veterans’ education benefits and issues regular updates affecting GI Bill users. Following the Supreme Court’s Rudisill decision, VA updated its guidance to reflect that certain veterans with multiple qualifying periods of service may access up to 48 months of combined education benefits, depending on eligibility circumstances [2]. VA also requires monthly enrollment verification for beneficiaries receiving housing or kicker payments under several chapters, with verification available by text, email, or phone; missed verification can delay payments [3]. For Yellow Ribbon, VA manages school agreements and states that schools wanting to participate or change contributions for the 2025–2026 academic year had to submit agreements by May 15, 2025 [4]. For on-the-job and apprenticeship training, VA explains that eligible beneficiaries can receive GI Bill support for approved OJT/apprenticeship programs, with payments generally decreasing over time as wages rise [5].

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
2. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Montgomery GI Bill and Post-9/11 GI Bill changes (Rudisill): https://www.va.gov/resources/montgomery-gi-bill-and-post-911-gi-bill-changes/
3. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Verify school enrollment: https://www.va.gov/education/verify-school-enrollment/
4. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Yellow Ribbon Program: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/post-9-11/yellow-ribbon-program/
5. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, On-the-job training and apprenticeships: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/how-to-use-benefits/on-the-job-training-apprenticeships/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA) and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), so both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses cover most major checklist items, including PII vs. non-PII collection, cookies, targeted advertising, data sharing, security safeguards, GI Bill administration, Rudisill updates, monthly enrollment verification, Yellow Ribbon participation, and OJT/apprenticeship support. However, both omit mention of the Perkins decision, automation of dual-eligibility reviews, and books/supplies coverage for OJT—so they are roughly equal in missed sub-points. That said, Agent B more clearly states that PII is collected when voluntarily submitted, explicitly notes the “no system is completely secure” disclaimer, and provides a specific Yellow Ribbon submission deadline (May 15, 2025), making it slightly more precise and concrete.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are readable and structured similarly, but Agent B provides more specific operational details (e.g., deadline date, clearer framing of voluntary submission and security limitations), which improves user confidence and practical utility. Since neither has major factual errors and both are strong, Agent B wins narrowly on clarity and specificity rather than correctness gaps.

Therefore, B is better, but not dramatically so.

logic_chain: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy
rounds.question: In the context of mastering the 7 stages of film production, aspiring filmmakers often rely on both specialized film schools and external funding sources. Identify (1) the prominent New York–based film academy known for hands-on training across the stages of production that publishes a digital privacy statement detailing how it handles personally identifiable information, cookies, advertising, and data security; and (2) the U.S. federal education benefits program that supports Veterans and their families in pursuing education and training (including film programs) under legislation such as the GI Bill. For each institution/program, provide a detailed explanation of their key policies or updates: For the film academy, summarize its policies on collection and sharing of personally identifiable information (PII), its use of cookies and targeted advertising, its data security measures, and how individuals can submit privacy-related questions (including the public contact channel and effective date of the policy). For the federal benefits program, describe recent updates affecting education benefits, including changes related to the Rudisill and Perkins court decisions, enrollment verification requirements introduced in 2026, the Yellow Ribbon Program update window, and any trademark notice associated with the GI Bill. Explain how these elements are relevant to a Veteran pursuing professional training in film production.
checklist_width: Film academy policy: PII not collected unless voluntarily provided
Film academy policy: PII not shared/sold to third parties; compliance with federal/state laws
Film academy policy: Use of cookies and targeted/interest-based advertising
Film academy policy: Security measures including encryption for sensitive data
Film academy policy: Public privacy contact email and effective date (May 21, 2018)
Federal program: Explanation of Rudisill and Perkins update (automation of eligibility review; no need to request dual assessment)
Federal program: Focus on Veterans with less than three months of remaining benefits
Federal program: Monthly enrollment verification requirement as of January 2026 (including methods such as text/email/online)
Federal program: Yellow Ribbon Program agreement window (March 15 to May 15, 2026)
Federal program: GI Bill trademark notice (registered trademark of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs)
checklist_depth: New York Film Academy + Identified as the New York–based hands-on film school with a digital privacy statement covering PII, cookies, advertising, and security
U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs Education Benefits (GI Bill program) + Identified as the federal program implementing Rudisill/Perkins decisions and 2026 enrollment verification updates
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by describing the entities indirectly: a New York–based hands-on film academy with a detailed digital privacy policy, and a federal Veterans education benefits program implementing specific court decisions and policy updates. This masking requires the agent to infer the correct institutions. It then requires wide aggregation by demanding multiple specific policy elements and legislative updates from both entities, ensuring the answer synthesizes scattered public information rather than relying on a single fact.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: VA » Veterans Benefits Administration » Education and Training Education and Training VA is implementing court decisions that allow additional entitlement to education benefit
rounds.answer_a:
New York Film Academy (NYFA) is the New York–based film school widely known for hands‑on training across all stages of film production. Its digital privacy statement applies to nyfa.edu and related online services. NYFA collects personally identifiable information (PII)—such as name, email, phone, address, date of birth, academic interests, and payment details—when users request information, apply, register, make payments, or otherwise submit forms on its sites.[1] PII is used to provide requested services, process applications and payments, manage enrollment, send marketing communications (where allowed), and comply with legal obligations.[1]  

NYFA states it may share PII with third‑party service providers (hosting, analytics, payment processors, email vendors), educational partners, and government or law‑enforcement agencies when legally required; it notes that it does not “sell” personal data in the traditional sense.[1] Non‑personally identifiable information—such as IP address, browser type, device identifiers, pages visited, and general location—is automatically collected via cookies, web beacons, and analytics tools.[1] Cookies are used for site functionality, remembering preferences, and analytics; users can control or delete cookies in their browsers, though some features may not function properly if cookies are disabled.[1]  

The policy acknowledges use of third‑party analytics and advertising partners that may support interest‑based advertising across websites, and refers users to browser settings and industry opt‑out mechanisms.[1] NYFA says it employs “reasonable” administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including encryption (e.g., SSL/TLS) for certain transmissions such as payment information.[1] Privacy questions can be directed to a designated email (e.g., privacy@nyfa.edu) and mailing address listed in the policy, which carries an effective date of May 25, 2018.[1]  

The relevant federal education benefits program is the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs’ GI Bill system of education benefits.[2] VA has issued updates following the Rudisill and Perkins decisions, expanding how multiple periods of service and certain eligibility disputes affect combined months of entitlement.[3][4] VA is also rolling out enhanced enrollment‑verification requirements in 2026, requiring more students (including non‑college and online learners) to verify enrollment monthly to keep housing payments flowing.[2] Yellow Ribbon Program participation is governed by an annual agreement window when schools must elect or renew Yellow Ribbon terms; VA publishes participating institutions and contribution limits each year.[5] VA also notes that “GI Bill®” is a registered trademark of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and specifies that schools must use the trademark with proper attribution.[6]  

For a Veteran pursuing professional film‑production training at NYFA, these elements determine (a) how their personal data and online activity are handled by the school, and (b) how much tuition, fees, and housing support they can receive, how long they can study under expanded entitlement from Rudisill/Perkins, whether their school participates in Yellow Ribbon for additional coverage, and their obligation to complete monthly enrollment verification to avoid interruptions in benefits.  

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy, “Privacy Policy,” nyfa.edu.  
[2] U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, “Education and Training – GI Bill,” va.gov.  
[3] VA, “Rudisill v. McDonough – GI Bill benefits update,” va.gov.  
[4] VA communications regarding Perkins‑related GI Bill eligibility updates, va.gov.  
[5] VA, “Yellow Ribbon Program – Information for Schools and Students,” va.gov.  
[6] VA, “GI Bill® Trademark Terms of Use,” va.gov.
rounds.answer_b:
The film academy is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), a New York–based school with hands-on filmmaking training across development, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution, and a published digital privacy policy for its websites and mobile services [1][2]. NYFA states that it collects personally identifiable information when users voluntarily provide it—for example through inquiries, applications, registrations, subscriptions, or other interactions. It uses that information to respond to requests, deliver services, communicate with users, and support school operations [3]. NYFA says it does not sell personal information, but may share it with service providers, affiliates, legal authorities, or in business-transfer situations described in the policy [3].

NYFA also automatically collects non-personally identifiable data such as IP address, browser and device information, operating system, usage patterns, referring pages, and pages viewed [3]. Its policy says cookies and similar technologies are used for functionality, preferences, analytics, and advertising; users can usually control cookies through browser settings, though disabling them may affect site performance [3]. NYFA also discloses targeted or interest-based advertising through third-party partners and points users to opt-out mechanisms [3]. For security, NYFA says it uses administrative, technical, and physical safeguards, including SSL encryption for sensitive transmissions, while acknowledging no online system is completely secure [3]. Privacy questions may be submitted through the contact channel listed in the policy, including the public email address privacy@nyfa.edu; the policy’s effective date is April 12, 2024 [3].

The federal benefits program is the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs’ education benefits system under the GI Bill and related programs [4]. Recent VA updates relevant to veterans pursuing film training include implementation of the Rudisill decision, under which some veterans with multiple qualifying periods of service may access up to 48 months of combined education benefits [5]. VA also references the Perkins decision in its current education-benefits legal guidance [6]. Beginning June 2026, VA will require monthly enrollment verification for students receiving MHA and/or kicker payments under Chapters 30, 33, 35, and 1606 [7]. For Yellow Ribbon, schools must submit or update participation agreements during VA’s annual window for the coming academic year [8]. VA also notes that “GI Bill®” is a registered trademark of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs [9]. Together, these rules affect how a veteran budgets tuition, maintains payment eligibility, and evaluates approved film-production training options.

## References
1. New York Film Academy, Stages of Film Production: https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/stages-of-film-production/
2. New York Film Academy, About / main site: https://www.nyfa.edu/
3. New York Film Academy, Privacy Policy: https://www.nyfa.edu/privacy-policy/
4. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Education and Training: https://www.va.gov/education/
5. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Montgomery GI Bill and Post-9/11 GI Bill changes (Rudisill): https://www.va.gov/resources/montgomery-gi-bill-and-post-911-gi-bill-changes/
6. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, education benefits legal/court updates referencing Perkins: https://www.va.gov/
7. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Verify school enrollment: https://www.va.gov/education/verify-school-enrollment/
8. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, Yellow Ribbon Program: https://www.va.gov/education/about-gi-bill-benefits/post-9-11/yellow-ribbon-program/
9. U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, GI Bill trademark notice: https://www.benefits.va.gov/gibill/trademark.asp
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified the New York Film Academy (NYFA) as the hands-on New York–based film school with a published digital privacy policy, and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (GI Bill program) as the relevant federal education benefits system. Both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both agents cover PII collection, sharing practices, cookies/targeted advertising, security safeguards (including SSL), a public privacy email, GI Bill trademark notice, Rudisill/Perkins updates, enrollment verification changes, and Yellow Ribbon participation. However, both miss several specific checklist details: neither clearly explains the automation of eligibility review and "no need to request dual assessment" element tied to Rudisill/Perkins, neither highlights the focus on Veterans with less than three months of remaining benefits, neither specifies the January 2026 enrollment verification requirement with methods (text/email/online), and neither provides the March 15–May 15, 2026 Yellow Ribbon agreement window. Both also provide incorrect effective dates for the NYFA privacy policy (ground truth: May 21, 2018). Thus, both have comparable WIDE gaps.

Presentation & User Experience: Agent B is more concise, better segmented, and slightly easier to scan. It separates institutional explanations more cleanly and integrates citations in a clearer structure. Agent A is more dense and text-heavy, with longer paragraphs that reduce scannability. Since factual performance is similar and neither has a decisive accuracy advantage, Agent B wins on formatting, clarity, and overall user experience.

Therefore, B is BETTER, primarily on presentation and readability rather than superior factual coverage.
